---
title: "Hello GitHub! <br> `r emo::ji('wave')`"
author: "S. Mason Garrison"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: "../slides.css"
    lib_dir: libs
    self_contained: TRUE
    nature:
      ratio: "16:9"
      highlightLines: true
      highlightStyle: solarized-light
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---

```{r child = "../setup.Rmd"}
```

```{r packages, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Remember to compile
#xaringan::inf_mr(cast_from = "..")
knitr::opts_chunk$set(knitr.duplicate.label = "allow")
library(tidyverse)
if (!require("emo")) devtools::install_github("hadley/emo")
library(emo)
if (!require("fontawesome")) devtools::install_github("rstudio/fontawesome")
library(fontawesome)
```

class: middle

# What even *is* GitHub?

---

## Learning Goals

By the end of this session, you will be able to...

- Describe what GitHub is and why people use it
- Navigate the GitHub website confidently
- Understand what a repository, README, and commit are
- Create your own GitHub account
- Create your first repository and make your first edit

--

.small[
No coding required today — just a web browser! `r emo::ji("globe")`
]

---

## A Problem You've Probably Had

.pull-left[
Have you ever saved files like this?

- `essay_draft.docx`
- `essay_draft2.docx`
- `essay_FINAL.docx`
- `essay_FINAL_v2.docx`
- `essay_FINAL_submit_this_one.docx`
- `essay_actually_final_FOR_REAL.docx`
]

--

.pull-right[
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align = "center", out.width = "90%"}
knitr::include_graphics("../d02_toolkit/img/phd_comics_vc.gif")
```
]

---

## We've All Been There

.pull-left[
This happens with:
- Lab write-ups
- Research papers
- Data analysis scripts
- Thesis chapters

.question[
What's the problem with this approach?
]
]

--

.pull-right[
- Which version is the "real" one?
- What changed between versions?
- Can you go back to what you had last Tuesday?
- How do you share it with a collaborator?
]

---

## There's a Better Way

**Version control** solves this problem by:

--

- Keeping **one file** (not 47 copies)

--

- Recording **every change** you make, with a description

--

- Letting you **go back** to any previous version

--

- Making **collaboration** easy — no more emailing files back and forth

--

<br>
.large[
**GitHub** is the most popular place to do this — and it's free!
]

---

class: middle

# GitHub is Just a Website

---

## Think of GitHub Like Google Drive...

.pull-left[
**Google Drive**
- Store your files in the cloud
- Share with others
- See who made changes
- Access from anywhere
]

.pull-right[
**GitHub**
- Store your project files in the cloud
- Share with collaborators (or the world!)
- See *exactly* what changed and when
- Access from anywhere
]

--

<br>
The big difference: GitHub is designed specifically for **projects with code and data**, and it keeps a **complete history** of every change ever made.

---

## GitHub is Used By...

- Scientists sharing their data and analysis code
- Software developers building apps and tools
- Writers collaborating on books and documentation
- **Psychologists** sharing reproducible research!

--

.question[
You don't need to be a programmer to use GitHub.  
You just need a web browser.
]

---

class: middle

# Let's Take a Look

---

## The GitHub Homepage

When you go to [github.com](https://github.com), you'll see:

- A search bar at the top — search for anything
- Trending projects — what people are working on
- Your feed (once you have an account)

GitHub hosts **hundreds of millions of projects** — from tiny homework assignments to the code that runs major software.

---

class: middle

# Hands-On Activity 1: Explore GitHub

---

.your-turn[

**Browse GitHub like a tourist** `r emo::ji("tourist")`

Open [github.com](https://github.com) in your browser.

1. Go directly to [github.com/hadley/r4ds](https://github.com/hadley/r4ds)  
   *(This is the repo for the "R for Data Science" book!)*
2. Scroll through the files — can you find the chapter files?
3. Click on `README.md` and read the first few lines
4. Find the commit count link near the top of the file list (it shows something like "**123 commits**") and click it
5. Click on any commit — what do you see?

⏱️ *Take 3 minutes to explore — no account needed yet!*

]

---

## What Did You See?

When you clicked on a commit, you probably saw something like this:

- Lines in **red** = text that was **removed**
- Lines in **green** = text that was **added**

--

This is called a **diff** — it shows exactly what changed.

--

.question[
Every single change to the book is recorded here.  
You can scroll back through *years* of history!
]

---

class: middle

# Three Key Ideas

---

## Idea 1: Repositories `r emo::ji("file_folder")`

A **repository** (or "repo") is like a project folder — but smarter.

It contains:
- All your files (data, scripts, documents)
- The **complete history** of every change ever made
- Notes about *who* changed *what* and *why*

--

Think of it as a folder that **remembers everything**.

---

## Idea 2: Commits `r emo::ji("camera")`

A **commit** is a saved snapshot of your project.

```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align = "center", out.width = "70%"}
knitr::include_graphics("../d02_toolkit/img/lego-steps.png")
```

Each step in building this LEGO set = one commit.  
You can always go back to any earlier step!

---

## What Makes Commits Special

```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align = "center", out.width = "70%"}
knitr::include_graphics("../d02_toolkit/img/lego-steps-commit-messages.png")
```

Each commit has a **message** — a short note explaining what changed and why.  
Future you will be very grateful for these!

---

## Idea 3: README Files `r emo::ji("memo")`

Every good repository has a **README** — it's the front page of your project.

A README usually explains:
- What is this project?
- How do I use it?
- Who made it and why?

--

README files are written in **Markdown** — a simple formatting language.  
You'll learn more about Markdown later in the course.

---

class: middle

# Create Your GitHub Account

---

## Sign Up for GitHub

.instructions[
Go to [github.com](https://github.com) and click **Sign up** in the top right corner.
]

Fill in:
- **Email address**
- **Password**
- **Username** — choose carefully!

--

**Tips for your username:**
- Use your real name or a professional variation (e.g., `jsmith`, `jane-smith`)
- You'll share this with professors and future employers
- Keep it short, lowercase, no spaces
- You can change it later, but URLs will break

--

.small[
Already have an account? Log in and follow along! `r emo::ji("check")`
]

---

## Verify Your Account

GitHub will send you a **verification email**.

1. Check your inbox (or spam folder)
2. Click the verification link
3. Complete the brief setup questions — or skip them

--

Once verified, you're in! `r emo::ji("tada")`

---

## Your GitHub Profile

Your profile page is like your **academic CV on GitHub**.

It shows:
- Repositories you've created or contributed to
- Activity over time (the green contribution graph)
- Projects you've starred `r emo::ji("star")`

--

.instructions[
Click on your avatar (top right) → **Your profile** to see your profile page.
]

---

class: middle

# Your First Repository

---

## Create a New Repository

.instructions[
Click the **+** icon in the top right → **New repository**
]

--

Fill in the form:

- **Repository name**: `hello-github`  
  *(use lowercase with hyphens — no spaces!)*
- **Description**: `My very first GitHub repository`
- Select **Public** (anyone can see it)  
  *(You can make it Private later)*
- ✅ Check **Add a README file** — always do this!

Click **Create repository** `r emo::ji("rocket")`

---

## You Now Have a Repository!

You should see a page with:

- Your repository name at the top
- A single file: `README.md`
- A green "Code" button
- Stats about commits and branches

--

.question[
This is your project's home on GitHub.  
Right now it only has one file — that's totally fine!
]

---

## What is in the README?

GitHub automatically created a `README.md` with:

```
# hello-github
My very first GitHub repository
```

The `#` makes a big heading. The next line is the description you typed.

--

Let's make it more personal! ✏️

---

## Editing a File on GitHub.com

You can edit files **right in the browser** — no software needed!

1. Click on `README.md` in your repository
2. Click the **pencil icon** ✏️ in the top right of the file view  
   *(It may say "Edit this file")*
3. You're now in an editor!

---

## Add Some Text

In the editor, add a few lines below what's already there.  
*(Replace MY-NAME-HERE with your actual name before typing!)*

```
## About Me

My name is MY-NAME-HERE.
I am studying psychology and learning data science.

## Why I'm Here

I want to learn how to share my research openly
and collaborate with other scientists.
```

---

## Save Your Changes with a Commit

Scroll down to the **"Commit changes"** section.

You'll see:
- A box for a **commit message** (already filled with something like "Update README.md")
- Make it more descriptive: `Add personal introduction to README`
- Leave "Commit directly to the main branch" selected
- Click **Commit changes**

--

.question[
🎉 You just made your first commit!  
Go back to your repository — your README now shows your new text.
]

---

class: middle

# Hands-On Activity 2: Make It Yours

---

.your-turn[

**Personalize your repository**

1. Go to your `hello-github` repository
2. Click the **pencil** ✏️ to edit `README.md`
3. Add at least **three things**:
   - Your name
   - Your program/year (e.g., "2nd year psychology PhD student")
   - One thing you hope to learn in this course
4. Write a descriptive commit message
5. Click **Commit changes**
6. Look at your repository's **commit history** — click on the commit to see the diff

⏱️ *Take 5 minutes*

]

---

class: middle

# What Comes Next

---

## You've Already Done a Lot!

Today you learned how to:

- ✅ Describe what GitHub is (a website for storing and tracking project history)
- ✅ Navigate a repository: files, README, commits, diffs
- ✅ Create a GitHub account
- ✅ Create a repository
- ✅ Edit a file and make a commit — all in the browser!

---

## Coming Up Next

In future sessions, we'll build on this foundation:

- **Connecting GitHub to RStudio** — work on your project locally and sync to GitHub
- **Cloning** — downloading a repository to your computer
- **Pull → Stage → Commit → Push** — the everyday workflow
- **Forking** — making your own copy of someone else's project
- **Collaborating** — working with others on the same repository

--

.small[
For now: don't worry about any of those words.  
Just remember: GitHub is a website where projects live. `r emo::ji("house")`
]

---

## The Only Thing to Remember Today

.large[
GitHub is like Google Drive — but for your research projects, and with a **complete memory** of every change you've ever made.
]

--

<br>

That's it. Everything else builds on that idea.

---

## Helpful Resources

- 📖 [Happy Git with R](https://happygitwithr.com/) — Jenny Bryan's guide for R users (we'll use this a lot)
- 🐙 [GitHub Docs: Getting Started](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started)
- 🎒 [GitHub Student Developer Pack](https://education.github.com/pack) — free pro features with your `.edu` email
- 📺 [GitHub Skills](https://skills.github.com/) — interactive tutorials on GitHub itself

---

# Sources

- Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel's Data Science in a Box ([link](https://datasciencebox.org/))
- Jenny Bryan's [Happy Git with R](https://happygitwithr.com/)
- [GitHub Docs](https://docs.github.com)
- PhD Comics [phdcomics.com](http://phdcomics.com)
